My research investigates how generative artificial intelligence reshapes normative behaviors, creative production, and labor market dynamics in the digital economy. I examine the intersection of AI technologies, platform governance, and creative labor through a lens grounded in information systems, behavioral economics, and copyright law. I draw on emerging copyright litigation involving generative AI firms as a natural experiment to understand the legal and economic tensions between AI training data practices and creator rights. These cases provide critical insights into the structural challenges facing creative workers and the policy interventions needed to address them.

My methodological approach combines large-scale computational analysis of creative artifacts, survey experiments measuring consumer and creator responses to AI-generated content, and policy analysis of emerging regulatory frameworks. I aim to produce findings that inform both academic debate and practical policy design.
